1:
"And it came to pass after these things, that the butler of the king of Egypt and his baker had offended their lord the king of Egypt." The witnesses note the offence of Pharaoh's butler and baker. On "the butler of the king of Egypt and his baker had offended their lord," the grammatical voices note the officers. Ellicott and Gill note that "the butler of the king of Egypt and his baker had offended their lord the king of Egypt" — Pharaoh's chief butler (cupbearer) and chief baker (high officers of the royal household) offended (sinned against, displeased) the king; the great officers fallen under the king's displeasure; the providence bringing them to the prison where Joseph was (vv.2-3), the occasion of the dreams.

On "had offended their lord," the puritan Henry and witnesses note the offence. Henry and Gill note the offence — some fault (perhaps suspected treachery, or some failure in their service) that angered Pharaoh; the displeasure of the king; the officers cast into prison (v.3).

On the offence, the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the providence: the offence and imprisonment of the two officers, by God's providence, brought them to Joseph's prison (vv.3-4), where their dreams (vv.5-19) and Joseph's interpretation would, in time, lead to his deliverance (41:9-13); the providence weaving the means of Joseph's exaltation. The verse displays the offence of Pharaoh's butler and baker.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the offence — "the butler... and his baker had offended their lord the king of Egypt," the officers' fault (Ellicott, Gill); "had offended their lord," the king's displeasure (Henry, Gill); the providence (41:9-13, Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the offending officers. The verse marks the butler: and baker. The verse displays the offence: the king. The verse displays the displeasure: of Pharaoh. The verse displays the providence: at work. The offence displays the officers brought to Joseph's prison. The verse displays the butler: fallen. The verse displays the baker: fallen. The verse displays the providence: weaving. The offence of Pharaoh's butler and baker displays the providence: the king's butler and baker offended their lord (41:9-13) — the great officers fallen, brought by God's providence to Joseph's prison, the occasion of the dreams and his deliverance. The verse displays the imprisoned officers (vv.2-3).
2:
"And Pharaoh was wroth against two of his officers, against the chief of the butlers, and against the chief of the bakers." The very brief verse notes Pharaoh's wrath against his two officers. The grammatical voices note the anger. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "Pharaoh was wroth against two of his officers, against the chief of the butlers, and against the chief of the bakers" — Pharaoh was angry with the chief butler (cupbearer) and chief baker (the heads of those departments of the royal household); the king's wrath at their offence (v.1); the great officers under the royal displeasure; the anger that led to their imprisonment (v.3).

On "the chief of the butlers, and... the chief of the bakers," the witnesses note the officers. Gill notes the two officers — the chief butler (who would be restored, v.21) and the chief baker (who would be hanged, v.22); the high officers; the heads of the royal service.

On Pharaoh's wrath, the grammatical-historical note the providence: Pharaoh's anger (the imprisonment of the two officers) was, by God's providence, the means of bringing them to Joseph's prison (v.3); the providence ordering the king's wrath to weave Joseph's deliverance (41:9-13); the divine hand in the affairs of kings. The verse displays Pharaoh's wrath against his two officers.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the wrath — "Pharaoh was wroth against two of his officers," the king's anger (v.1, Keil, Gill); "the chief of the butlers, and... the chief of the bakers," the two officers (vv.21-22, Gill); the providence (41:9-13, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the wrathful Pharaoh. The verse marks the wrath: against the officers. The verse displays the butlers: their chief. The verse displays the bakers: their chief. The verse displays the providence: at work. The wrath displays the officers brought low. The verse displays the Pharaoh: angry. The verse displays the officers: two. The verse displays the providence: weaving. Pharaoh's wrath against his two officers displays the providence: Pharaoh was wroth against the chief butler and chief baker (v.1) — the king's anger, by God's providence, bringing the officers to Joseph's prison (v.3), the means of his deliverance (41:9-13). The verse displays the imprisoned officers (v.3).
3:
"And he put them in ward in the house of the captain of the guard, into the prison, the place where Joseph was bound." The very brief verse notes the officers imprisoned where Joseph was. The grammatical voices note the prison. JFB and Gill note that "he put them in ward in the house of the captain of the guard, into the prison, the place where Joseph was bound" — Pharaoh imprisoned the two officers in the house of the captain of the guard (Potiphar, 39:1) — the very prison where Joseph was held (39:20); the providence bringing the officers to Joseph; the meeting that would lead to the dreams (vv.5-19) and Joseph's deliverance (41:9-13).

On "the house of the captain of the guard," the witnesses note the place. Gill notes that the prison was in the house of the captain of the guard (Potiphar, or his successor) — the state prison (39:20); the place of Joseph's imprisonment; the providence ordering the officers there.

On "the place where Joseph was bound," the grammatical-historical note the providence: "the place where Joseph was bound" — the officers brought to the very prison where Joseph was; the providence placing them under Joseph's care (v.4); the meeting that would, by the dreams, lead to Joseph's release (41:9-13); the divine weaving of the means. The verse displays the officers imprisoned where Joseph was.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the prison — "he put them in ward in the house of the captain of the guard... the place where Joseph was bound," the officers in Joseph's prison (39:20, JFB, Gill); "the house of the captain of the guard," the state prison (39:1, Gill); "the place where Joseph was bound," the providence (41:9-13, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the imprisoned officers. The verse marks the prison: the captain's house. The verse displays the place: where Joseph was. The verse displays the officers: brought there. The verse displays the providence: at work. The imprisonment displays the officers brought to Joseph. The verse displays the officers: imprisoned. The verse displays the Joseph: there bound. The verse displays the providence: weaving. The officers imprisoned where Joseph was display the providence: the officers were put in the prison where Joseph was bound (39:20) — the providence bringing them to Joseph, the meeting that would lead to his deliverance (41:9-13). The verse displays the officers under Joseph's care (v.4).
4:
"And the captain of the guard charged Joseph with them, and he served them: and they continued a season in ward." The very brief verse notes Joseph charged to serve the officers. The grammatical voices note the charge. Gill and Clarke note that "the captain of the guard charged Joseph with them, and he served them: and they continued a season in ward" — the captain of the guard (Potiphar, or the prison-keeper acting for him) charged Joseph to attend the two officers; Joseph served (waited on) them; the providence bringing the officers under Joseph's care; the meeting that would lead to the dreams (vv.5-19).

On "charged Joseph with them, and he served them," the witnesses note the service. Gill notes that Joseph was set over the officers (his trust in the prison, 39:22) and served them — the faithful, humble service; the godly young man attending the fallen officers; the providence placing him with them.

On "they continued a season in ward," the grammatical-historical note the time: "they continued a season in ward" — the officers were in prison some time; the season during which the dreams came (v.5); the providence preparing the occasion of Joseph's interpretation (and his deliverance, 41:9-13); the waiting. The verse displays Joseph charged to serve the officers.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the charge — "the captain of the guard charged Joseph with them, and he served them," Joseph serving the officers (39:22, Gill, Clarke); "charged Joseph with them, and he served them," the faithful service (Gill); "they continued a season in ward," the time (v.5, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the serving Joseph. The verse marks the charge: with them. The verse displays the service: rendered. The verse displays the officers: in ward. The verse displays the providence: at work. The charge displays Joseph attending the fallen officers. The verse displays the Joseph: serving. The verse displays the officers: in his care. The verse displays the providence: weaving. Joseph charged to serve the officers displays the providence: Joseph was charged to serve them, and they continued a season in ward (39:22) — the godly servant attending the fallen officers, the providence preparing the occasion of the dreams (v.5) and his deliverance (41:9-13). The verse displays the serving Joseph, with the officers' dreams (v.5).
5:
"And they dreamed a dream both of them, each man his dream in one night, each man according to the interpretation of his dream, the butler and the baker of the king of Egypt, which were bound in the prison." The witnesses note the butler and baker each dreaming a dream. On "they dreamed a dream both of them, each man his dream in one night," the grammatical voices note the dreams. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "they dreamed a dream both of them, each man his dream in one night" — the butler and baker each dreamed a dream the same night; the two dreams (vv.9-11, 16-17); the providence sending the dreams (as God spoke by dreams, Job 33:15; Numbers 12:6); the divine communication, each dream with its own meaning.

On "each man according to the interpretation of his dream," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the meaning. Calvin and Gill note that each dream had its own "interpretation" — a particular meaning (the butler's of restoration, the baker's of death, vv.12-19); the dreams not idle, but significant; the providence giving each its sense; the meaning that Joseph (by God) would declare.

On "the butler and the baker... which were bound in the prison," the puritan Henry and grammatical-historical note the providence: the two officers, in prison, troubled by their dreams — the providence sending the dreams to the very men under Joseph's care, that he might interpret them (vv.8-19) and, by the butler's later remembrance (41:9-13), be delivered; the divine weaving of the means of Joseph's exaltation. The verse displays the butler and baker each dreaming a dream.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the dreams — "they dreamed a dream both of them, each man his dream in one night," the two dreams (Job 33:15, Keil, Gill); "each man according to the interpretation of his dream," the particular meanings (vv.12-19, Calvin, Gill); the providence (41:9-13, Henry, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the dreaming officers. The verse marks the dreams: both. The verse displays the night: one. The verse displays the interpretation: each its own. The verse displays the providence: at work. The dreams display God speaking to the officers. The verse displays the officers: dreaming. The verse displays the dreams: significant. The verse displays the providence: weaving. The butler and baker each dreaming a dream displays the providence: each dreamed his own dream in one night, each with its interpretation (Job 33:15) — the dreams sent to the men under Joseph's care, that he might interpret them and, by the butler's remembrance, be delivered (41:9-13). The verse displays the troubled officers (v.6).
6:
"And Joseph came in unto them in the morning, and looked upon them, and, behold, they were sad." The witnesses note Joseph finding the officers sad. On "Joseph came in unto them in the morning, and looked upon them," the grammatical voices note the attention. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "Joseph came in unto them in the morning, and looked upon them, and, behold, they were sad" — Joseph, attending the officers (v.4), came in the morning and saw they were sad (troubled by their dreams, v.5); the observant, kind care of Joseph; the noticing of their distress; the occasion of his inquiry (v.7).

On "looked upon them," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and witnesses note the care. Wesley and Calvin note that Joseph "looked upon them" (observed them) — the attentive, compassionate care of the godly servant; the noticing of their countenance; the kindness that led him to ask the cause (v.7); the considerate Joseph.

On "behold, they were sad," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the distress: "behold, they were sad" — the officers troubled (their dreams, v.5, disturbing them, with no interpreter, v.8); the sadness Joseph observed; the occasion of his inquiry and the interpretation (vv.7-19); the providence. The verse displays Joseph finding the officers sad.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the attention — "Joseph came in unto them in the morning, and looked upon them... they were sad," Joseph noticing their distress (v.4-5, Keil, Gill); "looked upon them," the kind care (Wesley, Calvin); "behold, they were sad," the troubled officers (v.8, Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the attentive Joseph. The verse marks the morning: he came in. The verse displays the looking: upon them. The verse displays the sadness: observed. The verse displays the care: of Joseph. The attention displays Joseph's compassionate notice. The verse displays the Joseph: observing. The verse displays the officers: sad. The verse displays the care: kind. Joseph finding the officers sad displays his compassionate care: Joseph came in and saw they were sad (v.5) — the attentive, kind notice of the godly servant, the occasion of his inquiry (v.7). The verse displays the inquiring Joseph (v.7).
7:
"And he asked Pharaoh's officers that were with him in the ward of his lord's house, saying, Wherefore look ye so sadly to day?" The very brief verse notes Joseph asking the cause of their sadness. The grammatical voices note the inquiry. Gill notes that "he asked Pharaoh's officers... Wherefore look ye so sadly to day?" — Joseph, seeing them sad (v.6), kindly asked the cause; the considerate inquiry of the godly servant; the compassion that drew out their trouble (the dreams, v.8); the occasion of the interpretation.

On "Pharaoh's officers that were with him in the ward," the witnesses note the officers. Gill notes the officers in the ward (prison) with Joseph (v.3-4) — the fallen butler and baker; the men under Joseph's care; the troubled prisoners.

On "Wherefore look ye so sadly to day?" the grammatical-historical note the compassion: "Wherefore look ye so sadly to day?" — Joseph's kind, attentive question; the compassion for the troubled; the inquiry that led to the dreams (v.8) and the interpretation (vv.12-19); the considerate care that, by God's providence, opened the way to his deliverance (41:9-13). The verse displays Joseph asking the cause of their sadness.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the inquiry — "he asked Pharaoh's officers... Wherefore look ye so sadly to day?" Joseph's kind question (v.6, Gill); "Pharaoh's officers that were with him in the ward," the prisoners (v.3, Gill); "Wherefore look ye so sadly?" the compassion (v.8, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the inquiring Joseph. The verse marks the question: why so sad. The verse displays the officers: in the ward. The verse displays the compassion: shown. The verse displays the dreams: to be told. The inquiry displays Joseph's considerate care. The verse displays the Joseph: asking. The verse displays the officers: questioned. The verse displays the care: kind. Joseph asking the cause of their sadness displays his compassionate care: wherefore look ye so sadly today? (v.6) — the kind inquiry that drew out their dreams (v.8) and led to the interpretation (vv.12-19) and his deliverance (41:9-13). The verse displays the officers, who tell of their dreams (v.8).
8:
"And they said unto him, We have dreamed a dream, and there is no interpreter of it. And Joseph said unto them, Do not interpretations belong to God? tell me them, I pray you." The witnesses note Joseph ascribing interpretation to God — the crux of "interpretations belong to God." On "We have dreamed a dream, and there is no interpreter of it," the grammatical voices note the trouble. Ellicott and Gill note that "they said... We have dreamed a dream, and there is no interpreter of it" — the officers were troubled by their dreams (v.5) and had no interpreter (in the prison, away from the Egyptian magicians and diviners they would have consulted); the distress of the unread dreams; the occasion of Joseph's word.

On "Do not interpretations belong to God?" interpreters expound the saying. The wesleyan-arminian Wesley and the dominant reading note Joseph's great word — "Do not interpretations belong to God?": Joseph ascribed the power of interpreting dreams to God alone (not to the Egyptian magicians, nor to himself); God alone, the giver of the dreams (and of all revelation), can declare their meaning; the humility of Joseph (not boasting his own skill, but pointing to God, the revealer of secrets, Daniel 2:28); the godly disclaimer — interpretations are God's, and what Joseph would speak would be by God's gift (as he confessed before Pharaoh, 41:16, "It is not in me: God shall give Pharaoh an answer of peace"); the ascribing of all to God.

On "tell me them, I pray you," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the offer: "tell me them, I pray you" — Joseph offered to hear the dreams (confident that God would give the interpretation through him); the faith that God would reveal; the humble, God-honoring offer; the interpretation to follow (vv.12-19), by God's gift. The verse displays Joseph ascribing interpretation to God.

The witnesses agree the substance: the officers, having no interpreter, were told by Joseph that interpretations belong to God (Ellicott, Wesley, Calvin, Gill). The saying bears its weight as the crux of God the revealer: "Do not interpretations belong to God?" — Joseph ascribing the power to interpret dreams (and all revelation) to God alone, not to the magicians nor to himself; the humility that pointed to God, the revealer of secrets (Daniel 2:28; 41:16); the godly disclaimer, and the faith that God would reveal through him. The verse displays the interpretations-belong-to-God crux. The verse marks the trouble: no interpreter. The verse displays the saying: interpretations belong to God. The verse displays the humility: pointing to God. The verse displays the offer: tell me. The saying displays Joseph ascribing all to God. The verse displays the Joseph: humble. The verse displays the God: the revealer. The verse displays the faith: God will reveal. Joseph ascribing interpretation to God displays the crux: do not interpretations belong to God? (Daniel 2:28; 41:16) — Joseph ascribing the power to interpret to God alone (not the magicians nor himself), the humility that pointed to God the revealer of secrets, and the faith that God would reveal through him. The verse displays the offering Joseph, who hears the butler's dream (vv.9-11).
9:
"And the chief butler told his dream to Joseph, and said to him, In my dream, behold, a vine was before me;" The very brief verse notes the chief butler telling his dream of the vine. The grammatical voices note the dream. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "the chief butler told his dream to Joseph, and said... In my dream, behold, a vine was before me" — the butler recounted his dream: a vine before him (with three branches, budding, blossoming, bearing ripe grapes, vv.10-11); the dream of the cupbearer (fitting his office, the vine and the cup); the occasion of Joseph's interpretation (vv.12-13).

On "a vine was before me," the reformed JFB and witnesses note the vision. JFB and Keil note the vine — the central image of the butler's dream (the vine, the grapes, the cup, fitting his service); the dream Joseph would interpret (vv.12-13); the symbol of his restoration.

On the dream, the grammatical-historical note the providence: the butler's dream (the vine, vv.9-11), sent by God (v.5), would be interpreted by Joseph (vv.12-13) as restoration; the providence using the dream to lead to Joseph's deliverance (41:9-13); the means woven. The verse displays the chief butler telling his dream of the vine.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the dream — "the chief butler told his dream to Joseph... a vine was before me," the dream of the vine (vv.10-11, Keil, Gill); "a vine was before me," the central image (JFB, Keil); the providence (vv.12-13; 41:9-13, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the dreaming butler. The verse marks the dream: the vine. The verse displays the butler: telling. The verse displays the vision: before him. The verse displays the providence: at work. The dream displays the butler's vision of the vine. The verse displays the butler: recounting. The verse displays the vine: in the dream. The verse displays the interpretation: to come. The chief butler telling his dream of the vine displays the dream sent by God: a vine was before me (v.5) — the dream of the cupbearer (the vine and the cup), which Joseph would interpret as restoration (vv.12-13). The verse displays the dreaming butler (vv.10-11).
10:
"And in the vine were three branches: and it was as though it budded, and her blossoms shot forth; and the clusters thereof brought forth ripe grapes:" The very brief verse notes the three branches of the butler's vine. The grammatical voices note the vision. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "in the vine were three branches: and it was as though it budded, and her blossoms shot forth; and the clusters thereof brought forth ripe grapes" — the butler's dream: a vine with three branches, which budded, blossomed, and bore ripe grapes (the rapid growth in the dream); the three branches (the three days, v.12); the vine bearing fruit (fitting the cupbearer's restoration); the dream Joseph would interpret.

On "three branches," the reformed and witnesses note the number. Keil notes the three branches — which Joseph would interpret as three days (v.12); the symbol of the time to the butler's restoration; the detail of the dream.

On "the clusters... brought forth ripe grapes," the grammatical-historical note the fruit: "the clusters thereof brought forth ripe grapes" — the vine bearing ripe grapes (the butler pressing them into Pharaoh's cup, v.11); the dream of the cupbearer's service restored; the symbol of his restoration to office (v.13); the providence in the dream. The verse displays the three branches of the butler's vine.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the vision — "in the vine were three branches... the clusters thereof brought forth ripe grapes," the vine and its fruit (v.12, Keil, Gill); "three branches," the three days (v.12, Keil); "the clusters... ripe grapes," the restoration (v.13, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the dreamed vine. The verse marks the branches: three. The verse displays the budding: and blossoms. The verse displays the grapes: ripe. The verse displays the restoration: symbolized. The vision displays the butler's vine bearing fruit. The verse displays the vine: with three branches. The verse displays the grapes: ripe. The verse displays the interpretation: to come. The three branches of the butler's vine display the dream of restoration: a vine with three branches bearing ripe grapes (v.12) — the three days to the butler's restoration (v.13), the symbol of his service restored. The verse displays the dreamed vine (v.11).
11:
"And Pharaoh's cup was in my hand: and I took the grapes, and pressed them into Pharaoh's cup, and I gave the cup into Pharaoh's hand." The very brief verse notes the butler pressing the grapes into Pharaoh's cup. The grammatical voices note the service. Ellicott and Gill note that "Pharaoh's cup was in my hand: and I took the grapes, and pressed them into Pharaoh's cup, and I gave the cup into Pharaoh's hand" — the butler dreamed of doing his cupbearer's office: pressing the grapes into Pharaoh's cup and giving it to the king; the dream of his service restored (the very act of his office); the symbol of his restoration (v.13); the dream Joseph would interpret.

On "I took the grapes, and pressed them into Pharaoh's cup," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and witnesses note the act. Clarke and Ellicott note the act of the cupbearer — pressing the grapes, giving the cup (the ancient manner, or the dream's image of his office); the service of the butler restored; the symbol of his return to favor.

On "I gave the cup into Pharaoh's hand," the grammatical-historical note the restoration: "I gave the cup into Pharaoh's hand" — the butler serving Pharaoh again (in the dream); the symbol of his restoration to his office (v.13, "Pharaoh shall... restore thee unto thy place, and thou shalt deliver Pharaoh's cup into his hand"); the dream fulfilled (v.21); the providence. The verse displays the butler pressing the grapes into Pharaoh's cup.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the service — "I... pressed them into Pharaoh's cup, and I gave the cup into Pharaoh's hand," the cupbearer's office (v.13, Ellicott, Gill); "I took the grapes, and pressed them," the act (Clarke, Ellicott); "I gave the cup into Pharaoh's hand," the restoration (v.13, 21, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the serving butler. The verse marks the cup: in his hand. The verse displays the grapes: pressed. The verse displays the Pharaoh: served. The verse displays the restoration: symbolized. The service displays the butler's office restored in the dream. The verse displays the butler: serving. The verse displays the cup: given. The verse displays the restoration: to come. The butler pressing the grapes into Pharaoh's cup displays the dream of restoration: I pressed the grapes into Pharaoh's cup and gave it into his hand (v.13) — the cupbearer's office restored (the symbol of his return to favor, vv.13, 21). The verse displays the dreamed service, interpreted by Joseph (vv.12-13).
12:
"And Joseph said unto him, This is the interpretation of it: The three branches are three days:" The very brief verse notes Joseph interpreting the three branches as three days. The grammatical voices note the interpretation. Gill and Clarke note that "Joseph said unto him, This is the interpretation of it: The three branches are three days" — Joseph (by God, v.8) interpreted the butler's dream: the three branches (v.10) are three days; the interpretation given confidently (as from God); the meaning declared; the symbol read.

On "This is the interpretation of it," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the certainty. Calvin and Gill note that Joseph gave the interpretation with assurance (as from God, v.8) — not a guess, but the certain meaning revealed; the confidence of the God-taught interpreter; the meaning declared.

On "The three branches are three days," the grammatical-historical note the meaning: "The three branches are three days" — the three branches (v.10) signify three days (to the butler's restoration, v.13); the symbol interpreted; the time to the fulfillment; the providence revealing the meaning through Joseph. The verse displays Joseph interpreting the three branches as three days.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the interpretation — "This is the interpretation of it: The three branches are three days," the meaning given (v.8, Gill, Clarke); "This is the interpretation of it," the certainty (Calvin, Gill); "The three branches are three days," the symbol read (v.13, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the interpreting Joseph. The verse marks the interpretation: the three branches. The verse displays the days: three. The verse displays the meaning: declared. The verse displays the God: revealing. The interpretation displays Joseph reading the dream by God. The verse displays the Joseph: interpreting. The verse displays the branches: three days. The verse displays the restoration: to come. Joseph interpreting the three branches as three days displays the God-given meaning: the three branches are three days (v.8) — the symbol read (to the butler's restoration, v.13), the providence revealing through Joseph. The verse displays the interpreting Joseph, who foretells the restoration (v.13).
13:
"Yet within three days shall Pharaoh lift up thine head, and restore thee unto thy place: and thou shalt deliver Pharaoh's cup into his hand, after the former manner when thou wast his butler." The witnesses note Joseph foretelling the butler's restoration. On "within three days shall Pharaoh lift up thine head, and restore thee unto thy place," the grammatical voices note the restoration. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "within three days shall Pharaoh lift up thine head, and restore thee unto thy place" — Joseph foretold that in three days Pharaoh would "lift up the butler's head" (restore him, raise him to honor — the phrase used favorably here, but ominously of the baker, v.19) and restore him to his office; the interpretation of the dream (the three branches, v.12); the restoration foretold.

On "lift up thine head," the wesleyan-arminian and witnesses note the phrase. Gill notes the phrase "lift up thine head" — to raise to honor, restore to office (favorable here); the contrast with the baker (v.19, "lift up thy head from off thee," hanging); the same phrase, opposite issue; the restoration of the butler.

On "thou shalt deliver Pharaoh's cup into his hand, after the former manner," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the office: "thou shalt deliver Pharaoh's cup into his hand, after the former manner when thou wast his butler" — the butler restored to his cupbearer's office (as in his dream, v.11); the exact fulfillment (v.21); the interpretation that proved true; the providence (and Joseph's God-given gift) declaring the future. The verse displays Joseph foretelling the butler's restoration.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the restoration — "within three days shall Pharaoh lift up thine head, and restore thee unto thy place," the butler restored (v.12, Keil, Gill); "lift up thine head," the phrase (v.19, Gill); "thou shalt deliver Pharaoh's cup... as when thou wast his butler," the office restored (v.21, Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the interpreting Joseph. The verse marks the days: three. The verse displays the head: lifted up. The verse displays the office: restored. The verse displays the cup: delivered. The interpretation displays Joseph foretelling the restoration. The verse displays the Joseph: foretelling. The verse displays the butler: restored. The verse displays the fulfillment: to come. Joseph foretelling the butler's restoration displays the God-given interpretation: in three days Pharaoh will restore thee to thy office (v.11) — the butler's restoration foretold (fulfilled, v.21), the interpretation proved true. The verse displays the foretelling Joseph, who asks to be remembered (vv.14-15).
14:
"But think on me when it shall be well with thee, and shew kindness, I pray thee, unto me, and make mention of me unto Pharaoh, and bring me out of this house:" The witnesses note Joseph asking the butler to remember him. On "think on me when it shall be well with thee, and shew kindness," the grammatical voices note the request. Gill and Clarke note that "think on me when it shall be well with thee, and shew kindness, I pray thee, unto me" — Joseph, having interpreted the dream of restoration (v.13), asked the butler to remember him when restored, and to show him kindness; the lawful use of means (Joseph seeking, through the butler, his release); the request to be remembered; the godly man using means while trusting God.

On "make mention of me unto Pharaoh," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and witnesses note the appeal. Wesley and Calvin note that Joseph asked the butler to "make mention of me unto Pharaoh" — to speak of his case to the king; the lawful seeking of deliverance through the butler's influence; the use of the means God afforded (yet the butler would forget, v.23); the appeal for help.

On "bring me out of this house," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the deliverance: "bring me out of this house" — Joseph sought release from the prison; the desire for deliverance (the innocent longing to be free); the lawful means (not wrong to seek it, while waiting on God); the request that, though forgotten (v.23), would in God's time be answered (41:9-13). The verse displays Joseph asking the butler to remember him.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the request — "think on me when it shall be well with thee... make mention of me unto Pharaoh, and bring me out," Joseph seeking release through the butler (Gill, Clarke); "shew kindness... unto me," the lawful use of means (Wesley, Calvin); "bring me out of this house," the desired deliverance (v.23; 41:9-13, Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the requesting Joseph. The verse marks the request: think on me. The verse displays the kindness: asked. The verse displays the Pharaoh: to be told. The verse displays the deliverance: sought. The request displays Joseph using lawful means for release. The verse displays the Joseph: asking. The verse displays the butler: to remember. The verse displays the deliverance: sought. Joseph asking the butler to remember him displays the lawful use of means: think on me, shew kindness, and bring me out of this house — Joseph seeking release through the butler (yet forgotten, v.23), the godly man using means while waiting on God (answered in his time, 41:9-13). The verse displays the requesting Joseph, who pleads his innocence (v.15).
15:
"For indeed I was stolen away out of the land of the Hebrews: and here also have I done nothing that they should put me into the dungeon." The witnesses note Joseph's plea of his innocence. On "I was stolen away out of the land of the Hebrews," the grammatical voices note the wrong. Ellicott and Gill note that "I was stolen away out of the land of the Hebrews" — Joseph declared he had been stolen (kidnapped, by his brothers' sale, 37:28) from the land of the Hebrews (Canaan, where his fathers dwelt); the wrong done him (sold into slavery); the innocent man's account of his coming to Egypt; the plea of his unjust suffering.

On "the land of the Hebrews," the witnesses note the home. Gill notes "the land of the Hebrews" (Canaan, where Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob dwelt as Hebrews) — Joseph's homeland; the place from which he was stolen; the kindred of the patriarchs.

On "here also have I done nothing that they should put me into the dungeon," the reformed JFB and grammatical-historical note the innocence: "here also have I done nothing that they should put me into the dungeon" — Joseph protested his innocence (he had done no wrong to deserve the prison, the false accusation, ch. 39); the plea of the unjustly imprisoned; the innocent suffering wrongfully (1 Peter 2:19); the appeal to his blamelessness (both in being sold and in being imprisoned). The verse displays Joseph's plea of his innocence.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the wrong — "I was stolen away out of the land of the Hebrews," the kidnapped, sold Joseph (37:28, Ellicott, Gill); "the land of the Hebrews," Canaan (Gill); "here also have I done nothing that they should put me into the dungeon," the innocence (ch. 39; 1 Peter 2:19, JFB, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the innocent Joseph. The verse marks the stolen: from home. The verse displays the Hebrews: his land. The verse displays the innocence: done nothing. The verse displays the dungeon: undeserved. The plea displays Joseph's unjust suffering. The verse displays the Joseph: pleading. The verse displays the wrong: done him. The verse displays the innocence: protested. Joseph's plea of his innocence displays his unjust suffering: I was stolen from the land of the Hebrews, and here have done nothing to deserve the dungeon (37:28; 1 Peter 2:19) — the innocent man wronged in his sale and his imprisonment, the plea of his blamelessness. The verse displays the innocent Joseph, before the baker's dream (v.16).
16:
"When the chief baker saw that the interpretation was good, he said unto Joseph, I also was in my dream, and, behold, I had three white baskets on my head:" The witnesses note the chief baker telling his dream of the baskets. On "the chief baker saw that the interpretation was good," the grammatical voices note the encouragement. Gill and Calvin note that "the chief baker saw that the interpretation was good" — the baker, hearing Joseph's favorable interpretation of the butler's dream (vv.12-13), was encouraged to tell his own (hoping for a like good interpretation); the favorable reading emboldening him; the occasion of his dream's telling.

On "I also was in my dream... I had three white baskets on my head," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the dream. Calvin and Gill note the baker's dream — three white (or wicker) baskets on his head, the top one with baked goods for Pharaoh, the birds eating from it (v.17); the dream of the baker (fitting his office, the baskets and bread); the three baskets (the three days, v.18); the dream Joseph would interpret (ominously, vv.18-19).

On "three white baskets on my head," the grammatical-historical note the symbol: "three white baskets on my head" — the three baskets (the three days, v.18); the baked meats for Pharaoh (the baker's office); yet the birds eating (the ominous sign, v.17, 19); the dream whose interpretation would be death (vv.18-19), not restoration. The verse displays the chief baker telling his dream of the baskets.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the dream — "the chief baker saw that the interpretation was good... I had three white baskets on my head," the baker's dream (vv.12-13, Gill, Calvin); "saw that the interpretation was good," the encouragement (Gill); "three white baskets on my head," the symbol (vv.17-18, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the dreaming baker. The verse marks the dream: the baskets. The verse displays the baker: encouraged. The verse displays the baskets: three white. The verse displays the interpretation: to come. The dream displays the baker's vision of the baskets. The verse displays the baker: telling. The verse displays the baskets: on his head. The verse displays the omen: to come. The chief baker telling his dream of the baskets displays the dream (ominous): three white baskets on my head (vv.17-18) — the baker encouraged by the butler's good interpretation (vv.12-13), yet his dream would mean death (vv.18-19). The verse displays the dreaming baker (v.17).
17:
"And in the uppermost basket there was of all manner of bakemeats for Pharaoh; and the birds did eat them out of the basket upon my head." The very brief verse notes the birds eating from the baker's basket. The grammatical voices note the dream. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "in the uppermost basket there was of all manner of bakemeats for Pharaoh; and the birds did eat them out of the basket upon my head" — the baker dreamed of baked meats (for Pharaoh) in the top basket, and birds eating them out of the basket on his head; the ominous detail (the birds devouring the king's food, a sign of the baker's coming death, v.19, the birds eating his flesh); the dream Joseph would interpret as death.

On "of all manner of bakemeats for Pharaoh," the witnesses note the office. Gill notes the baked goods for Pharaoh (the baker's office, as the butler's cup, v.11); the food prepared for the king; the symbol of his service (yet undone by the birds).

On "the birds did eat them out of the basket upon my head," the reformed and grammatical-historical note the omen: "the birds did eat them out of the basket upon my head" — the ominous sign (the birds devouring the food, presaging the birds eating the baker's flesh, v.19); the dream's dark issue; the symbol of the baker's death and dishonor (hanged, the birds eating him, v.19); the interpretation to follow (vv.18-19). The verse displays the birds eating from the baker's basket.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the dream — "in the uppermost basket... of all manner of bakemeats... the birds did eat them," the ominous dream (v.19, Keil, Gill); "of all manner of bakemeats for Pharaoh," the baker's office (v.11, Gill); "the birds did eat them out of the basket upon my head," the omen (v.19, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the dreaming baker. The verse marks the basket: uppermost. The verse displays the bakemeats: for Pharaoh. The verse displays the birds: eating. The verse displays the omen: dark. The dream displays the ominous sign of the baker's death. The verse displays the baker: dreaming. The verse displays the birds: devouring. The verse displays the death: foreshadowed. The birds eating from the baker's basket display the ominous dream: the birds ate the bakemeats from the basket on my head (v.19) — the dark sign (the birds presaging the eating of the baker's flesh, v.19), the dream of death. The verse displays the dreamed omen, interpreted by Joseph (vv.18-19).
18:
"And Joseph answered and said, This is the interpretation thereof: The three baskets are three days:" The very brief verse notes Joseph interpreting the three baskets as three days. The grammatical voices note the interpretation. Gill notes that "Joseph answered and said, This is the interpretation thereof: The three baskets are three days" — Joseph (by God) interpreted the baker's dream: the three baskets (v.16) are three days; the parallel with the butler's three branches (v.12); the meaning given (though, this time, ominous, v.19); the symbol read.

On "This is the interpretation thereof," the witnesses note the certainty. Gill notes that Joseph gave the interpretation confidently (as from God, v.8) — the certain meaning; the God-taught reading; whether good (the butler) or ill (the baker), the truth declared.

On "The three baskets are three days," the grammatical-historical note the meaning: "The three baskets are three days" — the three baskets (v.16) signify three days (to the baker's death, v.19); the symbol interpreted; the time to the fulfillment; the providence revealing the dark meaning through Joseph (the honest interpreter, declaring the ill as well as the good). The verse displays Joseph interpreting the three baskets as three days.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the interpretation — "This is the interpretation thereof: The three baskets are three days," the meaning given (v.12, Gill); "This is the interpretation thereof," the certainty (v.8, Gill); "The three baskets are three days," the symbol read (v.19, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the interpreting Joseph. The verse marks the interpretation: the three baskets. The verse displays the days: three. The verse displays the meaning: declared. The verse displays the omen: dark. The interpretation displays Joseph reading the dream by God. The verse displays the Joseph: interpreting. The verse displays the baskets: three days. The verse displays the death: to come. Joseph interpreting the three baskets as three days displays the God-given meaning: the three baskets are three days (v.12) — the symbol read (to the baker's death, v.19), the honest interpreter declaring the ill. The verse displays the interpreting Joseph, who foretells the baker's death (v.19).
19:
"Yet within three days shall Pharaoh lift up thy head from off thee, and shall hang thee on a tree; and the birds shall eat thy flesh from off thee." The witnesses note Joseph foretelling the baker's death. On "within three days shall Pharaoh lift up thy head from off thee, and shall hang thee on a tree," the grammatical voices note the doom. Ellicott and Gill note that "within three days shall Pharaoh lift up thy head from off thee, and shall hang thee on a tree" — Joseph foretold the baker's death: in three days Pharaoh would "lift up his head from off him" (a grim play on the phrase used of the butler, v.13 — there to honor, here to behead/hang) and hang him on a tree; the doom of the baker; the interpretation (the three baskets, v.18); the death foretold.

On "lift up thy head from off thee," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the play. Calvin and Ellicott note the same phrase ("lift up thy head") used of both officers, but with opposite issue — the butler raised to honor (v.13), the baker's head lifted off (executed); the grim wordplay; the contrasting dooms.

On "the birds shall eat thy flesh from off thee," the wesleyan-arminian and grammatical-historical note the omen fulfilled: "the birds shall eat thy flesh from off thee" — the baker hanged, his unburied body eaten by birds (the very birds of his dream, v.17, now eating his flesh); the dishonorable death; the dark interpretation; the exact fulfillment (v.22); the honest, God-given reading proved true. The verse displays Joseph foretelling the baker's death.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the doom — "Pharaoh... shall hang thee on a tree; and the birds shall eat thy flesh," the baker's death (v.18, Ellicott, Gill); "lift up thy head from off thee," the grim play (v.13, Calvin, Ellicott); "the birds shall eat thy flesh," the omen of v.17 fulfilled (v.22, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the interpreting Joseph. The verse marks the days: three. The verse displays the head: lifted off. The verse displays the hanging: on a tree. The verse displays the birds: eating his flesh. The interpretation displays Joseph foretelling the death. The verse displays the Joseph: foretelling. The verse displays the baker: doomed. The verse displays the fulfillment: to come. Joseph foretelling the baker's death displays the dark, God-given interpretation: in three days Pharaoh will hang thee, and the birds eat thy flesh (vv.13, 17) — the grim contrast with the butler's restoration, fulfilled exactly (v.22). The verse displays the foretelling Joseph, with the fulfillment on the third day (vv.20-22).
20:
"And it came to pass the third day, which was Pharaoh's birthday, that he made a feast unto all his servants: and he lifted up the head of the chief butler and of the chief baker among his servants." The witnesses note the third day, Pharaoh's birthday, bringing the dreams to pass. On "the third day, which was Pharaoh's birthday, that he made a feast," the grammatical voices note the occasion. Ellicott and Gill note that "the third day, which was Pharaoh's birthday, that he made a feast unto all his servants" — on the third day (as Joseph foretold, vv.13, 19), Pharaoh's birthday, the king made a feast; the occasion on which the two officers' cases were judged; the providence bringing the dreams to pass at the appointed time; the royal feast.

On "Pharaoh's birthday," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and witnesses note the custom. Clarke and Ellicott note the birthday feast (the custom of kings, celebrating with a feast and acts of clemency or judgment); the occasion for the officers' sentencing; the day that fulfilled the dreams.

On "he lifted up the head of the chief butler and of the chief baker," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the fulfillment: "he lifted up the head of the chief butler and of the chief baker among his servants" — Pharaoh "lifted up the head" of both (the phrase Joseph had used, vv.13, 19) — to honor the butler (restored, v.21) and to behead/hang the baker (executed, v.22); the exact fulfillment of the dreams; the providence verifying Joseph's interpretation (and preparing his deliverance, 41:9-13). The verse displays the third day bringing the dreams to pass.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the occasion — "the third day, which was Pharaoh's birthday, that he made a feast," the fulfillment-day (vv.13, 19, Ellicott, Gill); "Pharaoh's birthday," the custom (Clarke, Ellicott); "he lifted up the head of the chief butler and of the chief baker," the dreams fulfilled (vv.21-22, Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the fulfilling day. The verse marks the day: the third. The verse displays the birthday: Pharaoh's. The verse displays the feast: made. The verse displays the heads: lifted up. The occasion displays the dreams brought to pass. The verse displays the Pharaoh: feasting. The verse displays the officers: judged. The verse displays the dreams: fulfilled. The third day, Pharaoh's birthday, bringing the dreams to pass displays the fulfillment: on the third day Pharaoh lifted up the heads of both officers (vv.13, 19) — to honor the butler and execute the baker, the dreams fulfilled exactly. The verse displays the restored butler and hanged baker (vv.21-22).
21:
"And he restored the chief butler unto his butlership again; and he gave the cup into Pharaoh's hand:" The very brief verse notes the chief butler restored, as Joseph foretold. The grammatical voices note the fulfillment. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "he restored the chief butler unto his butlership again; and he gave the cup into Pharaoh's hand" — Pharaoh restored the chief butler to his office (cupbearer), and he gave the cup into Pharaoh's hand (as in his dream, v.11); the exact fulfillment of Joseph's interpretation (v.13); the restoration foretold and accomplished; the providence verifying the word.

On "restored the chief butler unto his butlership again," the witnesses note the restoration. Gill notes the butler restored to his office (v.13) — the favorable issue (the "lift up his head" of honor, v.13); the dream fulfilled; the office regained.

On "he gave the cup into Pharaoh's hand," the reformed JFB and grammatical-historical note the office: "he gave the cup into Pharaoh's hand" — the butler serving Pharaoh again (the very act of his dream, v.11); the exact fulfillment; the interpretation proved true; the providence (and Joseph's God-given gift) verified. The verse displays the chief butler restored.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the fulfillment — "he restored the chief butler unto his butlership again; and he gave the cup into Pharaoh's hand," the butler restored (vv.11, 13, Keil, Gill); "restored the chief butler unto his butlership," the office regained (v.13, Gill); "he gave the cup into Pharaoh's hand," the dream fulfilled (v.11, JFB, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the restored butler. The verse marks the restoration: the butlership. The verse displays the cup: given. The verse displays the dream: fulfilled. The verse displays the interpretation: verified. The fulfillment displays Joseph's word proved true. The verse displays the butler: restored. The verse displays the cup: in Pharaoh's hand. The verse displays the interpretation: confirmed. The chief butler restored displays Joseph's word fulfilled: the butler was restored to his office and gave the cup into Pharaoh's hand (vv.11, 13) — the dream fulfilled exactly, the interpretation verified. The verse displays the restored butler, before the baker's hanging (v.22).
22:
"But he hanged the chief baker: as Joseph had interpreted to them." The very brief verse notes the chief baker hanged, as Joseph foretold. The grammatical voices note the fulfillment. Gill notes that "he hanged the chief baker: as Joseph had interpreted to them" — Pharaoh hanged the chief baker (as Joseph foretold, v.19); the exact fulfillment of the dark interpretation; the baker's death; the providence verifying Joseph's word (both interpretations, the butler's restoration and the baker's death, proved true).

On "he hanged the chief baker," the witnesses note the doom. Gill notes the baker's execution (v.19) — the fulfillment of the grim dream (the birds eating his flesh, v.17, 19); the dishonorable death; the dark issue.

On "as Joseph had interpreted to them," the grammatical-historical note the verification: "as Joseph had interpreted to them" — both interpretations exactly fulfilled (the butler restored, v.21; the baker hanged, v.22); the proof of Joseph's God-given gift (v.8, interpretations belong to God); the providence verifying the word; the interpreter vindicated (and the way prepared for his own deliverance, 41:9-13). The verse displays the chief baker hanged.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the fulfillment — "he hanged the chief baker: as Joseph had interpreted to them," the baker's death (v.19, Gill); "he hanged the chief baker," the doom (v.17, Gill); "as Joseph had interpreted to them," both dreams verified (v.8, 21, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the hanged baker. The verse marks the hanging: of the baker. The verse displays the dream: fulfilled. The verse displays the interpretation: verified. The verse displays the providence: confirming. The fulfillment displays both of Joseph's words proved true. The verse displays the baker: hanged. The verse displays the interpretation: confirmed. The verse displays the providence: vindicating. The chief baker hanged displays Joseph's word fulfilled: he hanged the chief baker, as Joseph had interpreted (v.19) — both interpretations exactly verified (vv.21-22), the proof of Joseph's God-given gift (v.8). The verse displays the verified interpreter, yet forgotten by the butler (v.23).
23:
"Yet did not the chief butler remember Joseph, but forgat him." The witnesses note the chief butler forgetting Joseph — the crux of the forgetting. On "did not the chief butler remember Joseph, but forgat him," interpreters expound the ingratitude. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note that "yet did not the chief butler remember Joseph, but forgat him" — the butler, restored to favor (v.21), did not remember Joseph (who had asked to be remembered, v.14), but forgot him (thought no more of him); the base ingratitude — forgetting his benefactor (the one who had interpreted his dream and comforted him); the all-too-common ingratitude of those raised to prosperity, forgetting those who helped them in adversity; the wrong done to Joseph (left in prison, his hope of release disappointed).

On the forgetting and Joseph's trial, the puritan Henry and the dominant reading note the providence: the butler's forgetting was a severe trial of Joseph's patience and faith — Joseph, who had looked for deliverance through the butler (v.14), was left in prison two full years more (41:1); the deferred hope; the trial of waiting; yet the providence in the delay — God's time was not yet (the butler must forget, that Joseph might be remembered at the right moment, when Pharaoh dreamed, 41:9-13, and so be raised not by the butler's favor but by God's hand, to the highest place); the lesson that man's forgetfulness cannot frustrate God's purpose (which used the very delay); the trial that taught Joseph to wait on God, not man (Psalm 118:8-9, better to trust in the LORD than to put confidence in princes); the providence overruling the ingratitude for good.

On the forgetting, the wesleyan-arminian and grammatical-historical note the warning and the comfort: the butler's ingratitude a warning (against forgetting benefactors); and a comfort (that God remembers his own, even when men forget, and delivers in his own time, 41:9-14); the trial of Joseph's faith, overruled by God. The verse displays the chief butler forgetting Joseph.

The witnesses agree the substance: the butler, restored, forgot Joseph, who remained in prison (Calvin, Henry, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of the forgetting: the base ingratitude of the butler (forgetting his benefactor, the common forgetfulness of the prospered toward those who helped them); yet a severe trial of Joseph's patience (two more years in prison, 41:1), overruled by the providence — God's time not yet, the delay used so that Joseph might be raised at the right moment by God's hand (41:9-14), not man's favor; the lesson to wait on God, not princes (Psalm 118:8-9). The verse displays the forgetting crux. The verse marks the forgetting: of Joseph. The verse displays the ingratitude: base. The verse displays the trial: of patience. The verse displays the providence: overruling. The forgetting displays the trial of Joseph's faith, overruled by God. The verse displays the butler: forgetting. The verse displays the Joseph: left in prison. The verse displays the providence: God's time. The chief butler forgetting Joseph displays the crux: the butler remembered not Joseph, but forgat him (v.14) — the base ingratitude, a severe trial of Joseph's patience (two more years, 41:1), overruled by the providence (God's time not yet, that Joseph might be raised by God's hand, 41:9-14, not man's favor), the lesson to wait on God, not princes (Psalm 118:8-9). The verse closes the chapter, before Pharaoh's dreams (ch.41).
